title: 2025/12/02 - GA [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Governance and Administration Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Public Service Commission [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Public Service Commission
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Governance and Administration Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Public Service Commission Public Service Commission - Sir Brian Roche, Public Service Commissioner - Heather Baggott, Deputy Public Service Commissioner - Alex Chadwick, Deputy Chief Executive Workforce – Workforce
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Public Service Commission. Public Service Commission. Sir Brian Roche, Public Service Commissioner

chunks.text: Focus on asking questions. It's not a bad idea.

Welcome to Brian, the Governance and Administration Committee and, uh, and to your team. Uh, we do have a short amount of time with you today, uh, to look, uh, at the Public Services Commission year in review. So, um, we'd like to invite you to make a few opening comments if you could keep that to under, uh, three minutes, and then we'll get into questions from members Over to you.

Hi, good morning, and thank you. The year to 30 June has been very busy, and the commission and our role as supporting ministers and chief executives across the public sector. Looking back, there are probably four things that I'd highlight. One, we spent a lot of time progressing the operating model that we believe appropriate for public sector.

Secondly, we've actively explored the role of digital and AI and the way we work. The third thing, uh, is the bargaining role that we've taken on teachers, which reflects a change in the delegations that have operated since the late 1990s. And the fourth thing is really supporting committee ministers on the changes to the Public Service Act.

So the commission itself is under surgical pressure. Uh, FTE head count is slightly lower than it was at 2017 for our core business, but we continue to perform and deliver to the best of our ability. Very happy to take any questions. Thank you. That the committee may have.

Thank you, uh, very much for that. Uh, I just wanted to start, if, um, I may in your, in your report at page 15, you note that it's the first time since 1997 that you've retained the collective bargaining delegation. Yes. In relation to a number of, um, collective bargaining negotiations that have been undertaken in the public service.

Uh, as part of that, uh, you've not only, uh, been responsible for bargaining, but you've also taken an active role in, um, as the kind of substitute employer in those bargaining practices. Uh, and that has recently, um, resulted in some controversy over some of the funding decisions that you've made in terms of the strategic direction that you're taking, that, that role of leading the bargaining in.

Uh, is there anything that, um, you wish to explain or to the committee around some of those decisions, specifically in relation to the, the ads that you did publish, um, on Facebook in relation to that bargaining role? The background to the commissioner, taking the role, the flex's strong desire for fiscal management across the public sector, and the best way to ensure that was direct involvement.

As I said earlier, that's, that's changed. Um, the bargaining process has been very challenging for all parties. I'm not suggesting that we were without fault, but we had a job to do, and we did that to the best of our ability. Um, the strengthened role in bargaining has taken a lot of resource, but as part of that role, it was very important to me and to the commission that we made sure that the facts were made available.

Social media is a critical channel by which people get their information these days. And so it was entirely appropriate in our view to participate in that. But I acknowledge it was the first time that we had done it in a bargaining context, and there was a lot of public commentary, which is why I initiated a review, um, immediately after the event itself, because I think it's important that we are both scrutinized and that we learn, um, it is likely that we will do this again.

And I wanted to make sure that we were as best as we could be, but I acknowledge it was the first time. And I also accept the public commentary. Do you, uh, accept that the Public Service Commission has a key role in upholding public sector neutrality as one of the key, um, pillars of our democracy in order to continue with public confidence within the public service?

I do. I also think it's the role of the public service to make sure that the right facts are getting to the right people. And I need to be able to take an active role in that when I see those facts being misinterpreted willingly or maliciously. Do you think that some of the advertisements that you published went a step further from simply providing facts and actually providing, uh, a perspective on the bargaining, um, against the striking workers and alongside, um, what would usually be the employer's role and you accept that, that may have impacted on, uh,

the public's perception of your neutrality? I don't accept that actually, but it's why I have initiated review to make sure that we do uphold the highest levels of integrity. There's no question about the need for that completely accepted, but as I said, the bargaining has been fraught this year, as has been very challenging.

It's been done in a very, very constrained fiscal environment. And from time to time, the information that was coming from the people we were negotiating with was not factual, and it was not accurate. And that is actually unhelpful to a broader functioning community. Do you accept, though, that the role of, um, trade unions and striking workers is not to, uh, not the same as your role in upholding public sector neutrality?

And so that might be the reason for the difference in what is appropriate for them may not be appropriate for you as Public Service Commissioner? I completely accept, and I've said publicly that the unions have a role. I'm not questioning their role at all. I just, I, I, I'll move on from this shortly, but I just wanted to give you the opportunity, um, from your perspective, the consultation that you undertook in relation to this particular ad campaign with ministers and minister's office, including the Minister of the Public Service and the Prime Minister, do you, uh,

feel that you consulted them effectively and that they were fully informed of the steps that you were intending to take? I think we were already on public record as saying that we could have done better in keeping them informed. They were decisions that I made. We should have kept them more fully informed, and we didn't.

And so some of the ministers, ministers were, as it were, caught short. I've acknowledged that. I'm not sure what all I can add to it, but it would be part of the learnings is if we're going to do this, the stakes were very high at the time, and I regret that ministers weren't kept as fully informed under the no surprises as they should have been.

The auditor, uh, general has now said that you were gonna undertake an external review and that they'll be looking at that review. Could you just, um, outline for the committee the steps that, um, have, uh, resulted in that particular decision and what, what, what we can expect to see in terms of reporting, public reporting on that review? I mean, from my perspective, as I said earlier, I thought it was really important given that it was the first time, and there's some learnings here, that we reviewed ourselves to make sure that we were at the standards

that we would legitimately expect a remain of that view. That work is underway, it has commenced, and when it's completed, I would hope to make that as available as I can, that I, I have got nothing to hide here. It's a very transparent process that Audit General has confidence in our ability to do that.

He, he is reserved to write, which I think is entirely legitimate to do further work. If he doesn't think we're up to the standard, he would require This f Final question from me, and then I've gotta start from Tim. The, if this external review indicates that the Public Service Commission did cross a line in publishing ads that essentially attacked to a certain extent the actions of striking workers, is that something that you will take into account and choose not to do in the future if it, if it is deemed that that wasn't an inappropriate step?

Absolutely. That's at the heart of the learning. There's no point in doing it unless you're able to listen to the conclusions and adapt your behaviors for the future. That's the essence of what I'm trying to do here. Yeah. So when you said earlier, you may do it again, that's dependent on the external review. Yes. But whether we like it or not, social media is a fundamental channel through which people obtain information.

We have to participate, but we recognize that there are standards of that participation and we need to make sure we haven't breached. Thank you. Yeah. I was just a, a couple of questions back. When we are talking about, I guess, how you negotiate within one particular case, the PPTA, um, when there are reports that they weren't putting the initial offer to their members, um, that not all members were given the, the chance to vote on an actual often before industrial election, is, is that a reason that you might need to go directly

through channels like social media in this instance, that, you know, if, if one side is playing those kind of games and potentially breaking the law, do, do you need the ability to communicate directly? I'm of the view that we do need to, we need to make sure that what we have been offering was made available to the members of the union.

If the executive of that union chose not to do it, that doesn't mean that we should just sit there passively. And so we worked very, very hard. The team worked very, very hard with PPTA, I was personally involved. I've got very clear recollection, recollection of what was said and what was agreed to Indeed, Uh, completely different area.

Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Um, thats Brian, one of the things that you've, um, talked about in your, um, overview in your, in the annual report is the whole thing about, uh, siloed thinking, uh, in across the public sector. Um, the one agency, which particularly has been set up to try and bring some silos together in sort of health welfare education area is the social investment agency.

And I just want to, uh, get your thoughts notwithstanding some challenges, Scott, at the moment, um, but, uh, to get your thoughts on how that work is progressing in terms of bringing together multiple agencies effectively through that, uh, multiple disciplines through that to try and get better outcomes for people that need those outcomes. Look, I'm very comfortable with the progress Social investment agency.

It, it is a new way of doing business, and it will add considerable value to the New Zealand taxpayer. So it makes a lot of sense, but it is going to take some time to completely change our model of operating. But you, you raise the point of fragmentation, fragmentation is a detriment to the system is very expensive.

It gives rise to duplication, and it doesn't always permit the public servants to do their best. So, social investment agency is part of a disruption factor. I think it is net positive. Um, and we've got some current issues that we're working through, but that does not undermine the intent of what they're doing.

So what do you see the future being for the social investment agencies see? Do you see it be becoming a bigger, uh, component of that, of those, um, areas which are currently sitting in the, the health, social development, um, uh, you know, education et cetera areas, and it taking a bigger role in those? I I, I do. I mean, it, it will evolve, to be honest.

We haven't got a perfect answer to that. But it, it is, it is a trial. It is an experiment, but it is clearly able to demonstrate significant value to the impact of the people that we're seeking to serve here, because it's actually not so much about how the agency is. It's is it making a difference in the lives of those who are most vulnerable and in need.

And it is. And I think that's good. Good. Excellent. Lawrence, Thank you. Uh, I wanna pick up on what, um, what you mentioned before. Welcome, by the way, um, in terms of providing the right information to, when we're looking at collective bargaining, I wanna pick up on the letter that you sent to NZDI, uh, and this is on the 4th of September.

Why have you chose to misrepresent information and budget in that letter when you said an extra $2.5 billion funding education, when the budget document itself actually does not state that the budget itself states an extra $1.8 billion in funding, not 2.5. I also want to pick up on the fact that you said that there's a nearly three quarters of a billion dollars in learning support across staff, when in fact it's $180 million.

When you do write letters like this to the union, do you get that independently fact checked? Of course I do. I would never knowingly mislead anyone in the information I provide. That's just an article of faith. To me, those numbers have all been checked by the Ministry of Education. I acted in completely good faith on that.

I think the broader point trying to be made to the NZDI and others was actually the investment in education is significant and should be acknowledged. How much of that extra three quarters of a billion dollars that you have quoted is actually going to schools. And the schools have control over that rather than a centralized process, which requires schools having to jump through bureaucratic hurdles to get access to the funding in the first place.

Because I think the thing is, you can represent the numbers, but I also want to pick up on the fact that at $2.5 billion, if you just even have a look at a summary of initiatives on page 30, it doesn't even say $2.50 on page 30 because you specifically said extra 2.5, it is 2.5, but $800 million at our clawback and our savings, that's not an extra.

So I understand that you are basing on the trust of what Ministry of Education giving you, but is there a process internally or would you consider now having a process internally when you do actually check some of the facts that figures? We, we, we do. Uh, I mean, a number of the questions you've asked, I, I'm not in a position to answer, but I can get them via the Ministry of Education.

I can certainly say we do have a process to check those as checked by the Ministry of Education. Those numbers came from the ministry. Um, they have not been challenged by, by the union, but as Brian said, that the point was to reflect that there is significant investment that has been made in this area. And it was to address some of the concerns that have been raised by the union and bargaining about that investment.

Supplementary to, um, can I just check that based on, this is the public service website, and then we are looking at department agencies, and particularly we are looking at chief executives for remuneration that there is an automatic 2% increase, including yourself. Um, there's been a 2% increase in your remuneration now at 401 K.

Do you see it ironic that you and chief executives in general public service gets independent remuneration authority to look at the remuneration, whereas we have seen support staff, for example, has been in collective negotiation bargaining with you for now coming up to 12 months.

Okay. Is that ironic? Firstly, um, my remuneration will sit by somebody completely outta my control. Yep, exactly. So I'm not sure what I'm supposed to do about that. Precisely. The, the Heather might wanna comment on, on the chief executors themselves, but we, we are seeking to get a balance between incentivizing and rewarding people.

Um, much of the bargaining that we have done, um, has been at above 2% for teachers, for instance. And that's a matter of public records. So I'm not quite sure what, what, Well, the question is things that were, that in terms of remuneration for both yourself and chief executives, like you said, are out of your control because that done by independent authority.

The question then is then why is the public service on public commission putting teachers and nurses and doctors through this incredibly stressful and un prolonged process? And like I said, support staff have been negotiation for up to 12 months now. They started in December last year.

I think education support staff is being made out of the Ministry of Education. That's not bargaining being led by the commission. But what I would add is that it's part of a collective bargaining process. This is the law. This is, this is the way that we need to negotiate collective agreements. It is something that we are required to do under the law. It is not of our choosing of how we set remuneration.

It is actually, um, uh, you know, a process that we must follow under the Employment Relations Act. It's also perhaps worth noting that, um, within the core public sector with the PSA, we have sealed 36 agreements and we have about 10 left. So it's, it, it's an unfortunate misrepresentation to think that we are not actually making progress.

Because even in the teachers, we have had the primary principals. Mm-hmm. We've got the secondary principals and we've just completed facilitation with PPTA. So there has been quite considerable progress. Mm. But up until then, what proportion, I mean, yes, I think the number of, of 36 with only 10 remaining is significant.

But those 10 remaining are also substantial in terms of the number of people they cover. So while you're saying 36 and 10 remaining, what proportion of that, in terms of actual number of people who are affected? I don't know exactly how many, but the nature of bargaining is actually, it involves two parties. We don't have automatic rights unless we're gonna throw a whole lot of money.

And that's not something we're going to do. We are looking for fear, equitable and affordable outcomes. And we have achieved that in a number of cases. But do you think that in this final, sorry. Thank you chair. Um, do you think in this case that in budget 2025, for example, we are currently, when you are negotiating, I understand the fiscal restraint that you are under.

However, what we've also seen, for example, in budget 20 20 25 is a significant investment, new investment of $9 billion into defense. The total investment we're looking at into defense is $10 million a day. So while you are operating fiscal restrain, and I understand, but have you also then considered why are we squeezing and then making our teachers and nurses and doctors and et cetera, having to settle for less when there's other significant investment in elsewhere?

Response, responsibility. That's the matters that you raised matters. They determine the apportionment across votes. I work within the constraints of that I was working and continue to work within a cabinet mandate and a purse. That's how the system works.

Okay, Tom, Uh, good morning. So Brian, in March of this year, you ran the public service con, uh, census and you went out to around 65,000 people who work in the public service. About 45,000 of them partake and responded through the census. One of the concerning parts that came back from it was only 44% of people who engaged in the census said they believed someone was hired based on merit.

Meaning 56% of people don't think people hired in the public service are based, are hired on merit. Um, can you give me sort of your analysis of that initially, please? Um, yes, it is quite concerning. 'cause as you know, um, merit based appointments is a really important fundamental principle. Is That the most important?

It's one of the top, um, priorities. Um, and we have been considering very seriously what else we can do to, um, emphasize it is an important principle. Um, I mean, there has been a lot of work in the space to ensure, um, recruitment and promotion policies and practices reflect those principles.

And there there is contestability, uh, in relation to chief executive appointments. Um, you'll be aware we have a quite a robust process there. Um, part of the rationale behind the changes to the Public Service Act, which removes the ability for reappointment, was a perception of, um, questions around the merit based nature of that.

And so we've adopted a practice in the meantime that all chief executive appointments will be a contestable process, not reappointments, uh, in preparation for the act coming into place. The only other exception is when a transfer is required, uh, that's a transfer of a chief e from one role to the other. Mm-hmm. Um, when we do that, that's on a very exceptional circumstances.

And even then, we will run a merit based assessment of their suitability to do the other job. So more work for us to do, answering what additional policy guidelines that we can provide across the wider public Sector. But there is a concern that people believe that because what they believe will, will be the truth in their minds.

And what we, we have to do more to demonstrate that there's never a question on the issue of merit. Now, wouldn't it be the aim of the, thank you for the answer. Wouldn't it be the aim of the public service commission to want to have 90, 95, a hundred percent of people who work there thinking that people are hired based on merit and not on any other external factor, eg.

Whether someone is hired based on their ethnicity, um, their sexuality, their gender, shouldn't it be the right person for the job, the right role to lead that team? Uh, absolutely everything should be based on merit. And in particular for chief executive appointments, that we are impartial and they are not influenced in any political way.

Mm-hmm. Um, we have a positive duty to ensure that, um, for all our roles in the sectors right, through the whole of our workforce. What can you do to lift that percentage for people? So the people who work in the public service to say, we've taken your concerns on board. We are doing something about making sure it's merit based, but we want to ensure that, you know, that you've been listened to improve that perception around that people are solely hired based on merit.

Uh, We, we have to stick to the standards that we set for ourselves. Yeah. And be very transparent about it. We, we think it's corrosive to the system if there is either any doubt. Mm-hmm. We also think it's poor for the incumbent not to have the confidence that they were the best person for the job.

Agreed. So there's actually more than one factor that we are playing into. But I, you know, I'm not dismissing that's how they feel. That's what they think. We have to respond to it as we move to a whole lot of the other matters. In the survey, we have supplementary Lawrence. Thank you. Um, it's good to hear that.

But in the latest census, for example, that Asians now make up 70.2% of the population, yet in tier one to three public service roles, they make up less than 2%. Then do you think that Asians do not have merit? And are we planning on addressing that, that discrepancy? Um, sure. We absolutely do not think that.

Um, we have a, um, a clear diversity strategy across the public service and we had, have, have had for a long time. And that's focused on ensuring that we attract the diversity of talent and retain them and progress them through every level of the public service. Um, and, and that we can ensure that we are delivering high performing public services as a result. Um, our strategy particularly, um, looks at valuing diversity of thought and experience at all levels, um, attracting the widest pool of candidates, and that includes into those senior roles,

um, and applying the merit principle comprehensively. And we are working on that at not only at the appointment stage, but at every stage of the employment life cycle. So, um, we are recognizing, we do recognize that there is further work to do in a number of areas to bring, um, you know, a broader set of applicants through. Um, and that is a continued pro progress.

One more supplementary just on this. In terms of lower tiered roles, we see that there's a broader representation of Asians in the public service, usually 10 plus percent, but we do not see that progressing onto tier one to three managers for a number of years. So why it's having to say that there's a strategy, but we're not seeing that happening in action. Why is that? We have seen in recent years in our data and in, um, an increase in younger, um, public servants coming into the public service, which is one of the reasons for that increase in, um, people in those,

um, lower and more entry level roles. Um, we are working very hard with agencies to support people to progress, um, into those senior roles, but it does take time. Can I just add to that, just in terms of the numbers, but year on year over the last sort of almost 15 years, we've seen a rep an increase each year in the number of Asian representation in the public service.

At the moment, it's 16.5%, so it's quite high, high. Um, and as you point out with the new recruits coming into the public service, that was 18.3% in the last year. So we are seeing as, as a shifting demographic graphics more coming through, and this is particularly pronounced in Auckland, where Asian staff make up 30.7% of our employees in 2025.

So as Alex has pointed out, as we see more recruits coming through through, we expect over time they'll make their way up into senior leadership roles. And I think if I just add from the commission's perspective, we are actively focused on how we make the public service a genuine career option for people who seek to serve general public.

And that is gonna require us to think much more clearly about how, how do we make this a career for people that they benefit from. And in doing so, they do, they contribute to New Zealand because one of the very positive aspects of the census was that people understand the purpose of their work.

That's a very important asset. Thank you. Melissa. Just A supplementary in relation to the Asian population within the public service, Asian pay gap is the largest among ethnic pay gap at 13.8% in 2024. Uh, do you think that's fair or is there a reason? Is it because we have a young population of public service? Yes. Who are starting Primarily it is because the younger cohort are in those more junior levels, um, junior level roles, and that is what is contributing to that pay gap.

Um, it has decreased to 13% from 13.8 in 2024, small reduction. So we are seeing a little bit of movement there, but primarily it is, it is where it is because of the composition across, um, those different roles. Thank you. I just wanna, um, pick up a couple of points in relation to, um, your annual report and the commission's workload.

But just to, um, follow up from a comment that you made before around consulting with ministers. Uh, prior you said that you thought that you could have done better consulting with ministers in relation to the ads. Um, uh, answer and answers to written question. It appears that the draft media strategy, media statements, social media tiles, and an memoir received by the Prime Minister's office, the Public Service Minister's office, and the education minister's office in advance of that strategy.

So what in addition to that do you think the Public Service Commission could have done to inform ministers? We should Of the Process Highlighted. Sorry. We should have highlighted key factors. And we didn't, we didn't, making a paper available as part of a consultation. I think there's also a professional obligation to highlight issues which are new or sensitive, and we failed to do that and we should have done better.

So just, um, linking that to, um, something that you've put in your report. Um, in your report you say that, uh, the three intended performance improvement reviews were not completed, um, this year due, um, to resources being, uh, directed towards other priorities. Uh, and the same time, um, you've had an increase in head count.

So do you, do you have the ability with the ministerial priorities, and I'm happy for you to elucidate on what those ministerial priorities are, but with this bargaining work that you are taking on, and with all this additional work that you, that you seem to have, it seems that you're not being able to fulfill the four functions that the public Service commissioner, um, stated that you would, um, like to be able to achieve.

So can you let us know, um, why, why that is the case? Uh, relation to the chief executive performance expectations and performance reviews? Uh, we do everything we can to try and meet those targets, but on occasion, we work precious demand. We are not able to do so. Um, and sometimes when we have acting chief executives, um, it's more important we take the time to be really clear about what is required rather than meeting the specific deadline.

But absolutely, we didn't quite make that measure this year, and we will be focusing on doing that next year. Hmm. And so what were the, um, ministerial priorities that took you away from doing that, that core part of the Public Service Commission's work? There's a, there's a range of things that keep us busy. Um, I can't recall in the moment, but there will be multiple things we're juggling at, at any given time.

But in particular, the work, um, that, uh, so Brian has mentioned around, um, the operating model, uh, for the public sector. That's been a big focus for us at the Public Service Commission, um, and giving advice our team, Melissa, about how best to achieve that. Just wanted to ask, um, uh, sir Brian, you are the employer or the chief executives within the public service, and there's been some high profile integrity challenges that have been noted, um, during the past year in the public sector.

Um, how are you seeking to Dr. Address this in terms of any changes to processes or procedures to try and ensure that these these issues do not occur in the future? Heather, Heather might wanna comment, but, um, maintaining the integrity and the public trust and confidence is very, very important to us.

We have had a wake up call, particularly if you're referring to given McKim issue, that, as I've said publicly, that was a wake up call. We had it reviewed, there was suggestions made to us about how we can improve. We adopted those immediately, and in fact, we've strengthened them. And so, as I've also said publicly, we're gonna continue to work sort of to the best of our ability to make sure we are at international best practice, because this is a rapidly moving, um, issue.

The, the nature of what people do in their private lives and its impact on their ability to hold public office is a serious issue for us. It's a serious issue for New Zealand society, actually. So I don't know whether you wanted to add anything. I just, um, I might comment on the more broader point about what we're doing around integrity across the public sector.

My more recently, um, the public service code of conduct was reissued, which sets out what those code, the code and the expectations on all public servants are. Um, we continue to have a, a, a stringent focus on this, which is a key part of that is every agency ensuring that they have key leaders, integrity champions, who ensure that their agency are hearing to the standards that are expected on them, um, right through to our investigation and inquiry, um, duties.

So if there is a serious breach, we expect every chief executive, for example, the leaking off of, um, or unauthorized disclosure of materials. We expect chief executives to take that very seriously and look into those matters immediately and get to the bottom, not them. And if something reaches a, um, significant threshold where there has been a quite a serious breach around integrity, then of course the Public Service Commission has the ability to do an inquiry.

And we do use that lever when that's necessary. But we also, uh, on record integrity as an idea is under challenge just generally. So we are actually doing our best to strengthen our guidance and our rules. So it is very clear we're also introducing more education of public servants around what they should be doing.

And we want keep that very current. So this, as I said earlier, it's, it's an evolving, it's an evolving theme at the moment for us, and one that we need to get ahead of if we're going to be successful. Uh, Andy? Yeah. Yes, I do. Um, thanks. But the, um, one of the things you talked about last time, um, you were here was the, the essentially the size as a whole, um, and the work that you were doing.

Then obviously there was some initial, um, reductions in the head count. Um, but when they, when this government came in, uh, you indicated there was further work going on. Um, I'm just interested to, to hear how that's progressing and also how that relates to, and I, I remember you, you talked about AI as, as part of that, you've also talked again today about the breaking down the silos and trying to make the system more efficient because it's not, not efficient at the moment.

So how that improving the efficiency and effectiveness of public sector is going and what that means for, uh, the number of people that we need to do the job. We don't have a targeted headcount number. That's fine. We, we do have a target about improving the efficiency and effectiveness mm-hmm. Of the public sector. Um, I have spoken and worked extensively with the chief executives this year over, you know, how do we get a model that's better fit for the future and better serves the people that we're seeking to serve?

There's a view, um, that there are too many agencies, some of them are below critical mass and therefore aren't providing the career options or resilience that we would expect. So there's still too much duplication in some areas. We believe that we could actually maintain the same level of services, but with less entities.

And that's the hypothesis that we are now actively exploring. We've, we are doing the work, ultimately this will be a matter for ministers to determine. We don't have that. But we believe that the model that we've had, which has been hardwired, uh, into silos for the best part of 40 years is no longer fit for the future.

We think there is merit in exploring how we could change that. What's the role of digital? What's the role of ai? How are we going to respond to the fiscal pressures? And as I said earlier, more importantly, how are we gonna maintain the, the human capital and make that a real asset for us?

Because it is a real asset and the census has proved that the quality of our people is very, very good and we need to have that further enhanced and respected. So you're making progress in terms of thinking about the right structure and, and, um, trying to reduce duplication. Do you have a sort of timeframe of which we are likely to see some of those thoughts, um, come to, uh, come to everybody's attention?

I think maybe in the next 12 months. But what whatever we do has to be done in a way we will maintain business as usual. Yeah.
